titleStmt.title: An improved model for describing the net carrier recombination rate in semiconductor devices

funder.orgName: Ministry of Education and Science of the Russian Federation
Ministry of Higher Education of Malaysia

analytic.title: An improved model for describing the net carrier recombination rate in semiconductor devices

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: Carrier recombination is a process that significantly influences the performance of semiconductor devices such as solar cells, photodiodes, and light-emitting diodes (LEDs).

Therefore, a model that can accurately describe and quantify the net carrier recombination rate in semiconductor devices is important in order to further improve the performance of relevant semiconductor devices. The conventional model for describing the net carrier recombination rate is derived based on the condition that there is no electric current in the considered semiconductor, which is true only when the semiconductor is not part of a semiconductor device, and hence is not connected to an external circuit. The conventional model is adopted and used for describing the net carrier recombination rate in semiconductors that are part of devices (i.e. in semiconductor devices). In this paper, we derive and propose a new model for describing the net carrier recombination rate in semiconductor devices. The newly proposed model is an improvement to the currently used model by considering the fact that electric current can flow in the semiconducting materials of semiconductor devices. We validate the 2 proposed recombination model and show that the use of the proposed model can be crucial for modeling and analyzing the performance of optoelectronic devices such as solar cells and LEDs.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Recombination of electrons and holes is a fundamental process that occurs in a semiconductor whether the semiconductor is part of a semiconductor device or not. Carrier recombination is of special interest to several semiconductor devices. In solar cells and photodiodes, all carrier recombination processes should be minimized, whereas in light-emitting diodes (LEDs), radiative recombination should be maximized, and other possible recombination processes should be minimized. Therefore, an accurate description and quantification of the carrier recombination rate is important to accurately identify and evaluate the possible areas of improvement for those devices. An accurate quantification of the carrier recombination rate could also be important for studies on the fundamental properties of semiconductors.
For a given carrier recombination process, the net carrier recombination rate per unit volume net R in a semiconductor is given by [1, pp. 313-324]
div.formula: net total 0 R R G =-,(1)
div.p: where total R is the total carrier recombination rate per unit volume in the semiconductor, and 0 G is the carrier generation rate per unit volume that inherently or permanently occurs in the semiconductor. In a semiconductor that is not part of a device (called an isolated semiconductor here), it is well known that 0 G originates from thermal agitation, which causes electrons in the valence band jumping either directly or indirectly into the conduction band, thus producing electrons in the conduction band and electron vacancies (holes) in the valence band [1, pp. 313-324]. In a semiconductor that is part of a device, there is an additional factor that contributes to 0 G as will be shown in this paper.

The conventional model for describing the detail expression of net R is derived under the condition that there is no electric current in the considered semiconductor, which is true only when the semiconductor is not part of a device and not connected to an external circuit.

However, this conventional model is adopted and widely used to describe net R even in semiconductors that are part of devices (i.e., in semiconductor devices). In this paper, we derive a new model for describing net R in semiconductor devices and show that it should be different compared with the conventional net R model. The newly proposed model is an improvement to the currently used model by considering the fact that electric current can flow in semiconductors that are part of devices. We validate the proposed model and show that the use of the proposed model is important to better analyze and enhance the performance of relevant semiconductor devices.

This paper is organized as follows. In Sec. 2, we describe the carrier continuity equations and the carrier recombination in semiconductors. In Sec. 3, first we rederive the conventional model for quantifying net R , and then we derive a new improved model for quantifying net R in semiconductor devices. In Sec. 4, we employ organic solar cells (OSCs) as a case study to validate and demonstrate the significance of the newly proposed model. In Sec.
4, we also provide some physical insights regarding the newly proposed net R model. The paper is then concluded in Sec. 5.

div.head: Carrier continuity equations and carrier recombination in semiconductors
div.p: The continuity equations for electrons and holes in semiconductors are given by [1, pp. 58-63,
div.formula: p p p 1 p GR qt  -  + -=  J , (3
div.p: where q is the elementary charge, n J ( p J ) is the electron (hole) current density, n G ( p G ) is the electron (hole) generation rate per unit volume due to photon absorption, n R ( p R ) is the net electron (hole) recombination rate per unit volume, n ( p ) is the density or concentration of free electrons (holes), and t is time. Carrier generation due to external photon absorption is not a source of carrier generation that inherently occurs in semiconductors, and therefore n G and p G have their own separate terms in the continuity equations (i.e. are not included in n R and p R ). For the one-dimensional case at steady state, the continuity equations become [2, p.
n nn
div.formula: p pp 1 0 J GR qx  - + -=  . (5
div.p: The electron current density n J and the hole current density p J for the one-dimensional case are given by n n n dn J q Fn qD dx
div.formula: p p p dp J q Fp qD dx  =- ,(7)
div.p: where n  ( p  ) is the electron (hole) mobility,
div.formula: F is the electric field, n n B D k T q  = is the electron diffusion coefficient, and p p B D k T q  = is the hole diffusion coefficient, where B k
div.p: is the Boltzmann constant and T is absolute temperature. Here, we consider the case where there is no temperature gradient (i.e., 0 dT dx = ) since the effect of the temperature gradient is negligible in general semiconductor devices (hence the current densities consist of the drift and the diffusion components only), unlike in thermoelectric generators where the temperature gradient is purposely maintained.

In general, there are three main recombination mechanisms, which are the band-to-band (also called radiative) recombination, Shockley-Read-Hall (SRH) or trap-assisted recombination, and Auger recombination [1, pp. 39-44]. The total band-to-band recombination rate is proportional to the product of the electron and the hole concentrations (i.
div.formula: R k np G =-,(8)
div.p: where bb k is the band-to-band recombination coefficient and
div.formula: G k n p = .
div.p: The total SRH recombination rate is also proportional to np [1, pp. 317-324].
Therefore, the net SRH recombination rate per unit volume is given by [1, pp. 317-324] ( )
div.formula: SRH SRH 0(SRH) R k np G =-,(10)
22 A A 0(A) R k n p p n G = + - ,(12)
div.p: where A k is the Auger recombination coefficient and 0(A) G is the 0 G component for the Auger recombination process. To be consistent with bb R and SRH R , we can write Eq. ( 12) as [1, pp.

div.formula: A A A 0(A) 0(A) R k n k p np n p = + - ,(13) which means
0(A) A 0(A) 0(A) G k n p n p =+ . Note that Eq. (13) indicates that 0(A) G also changes if n G and p G change.
div.p: The net electron and the net hole recombination rates per unit volume (i.e., n R and p R
) are sum of all possible processes, and hence are
div.formula: n p bb SRH A R R R R R = = + + . (14
div.p: From Eq. ( 14), it is also easy to see that the total carrier recombination rate is the sum of bb k np

div.head: Model for quantifying the net carrier recombination rate in isolated semiconductors
div.p: Here, an isolated semiconductor is referred to a semiconductor, intrinsic or extrinsic (doped), that is not part of a semiconductor device, and hence it is not connected to an external circuit and no current can flow inside it. To determine 0(bb) G , 0(SRH) G , and 0(A) G , we need to determine 0(bb) n , 0(bb) p , 0(SRH) n , 0(SRH) p , 0(A) n , and 0(A) p . Let us consider a special case when n R and p R are zero. Let us denote the electron concentration n and the hole concentration p in isolated semiconductors when np 0 RR == as 0(is) n and 0(is) p , respectively. As mentioned in Sec. 2, when n R and p R are zero, bb R , SRH R , and A R must all be zero, and hence according to Eqs.
(9), (11), and (13), we must have
The result given by Eqs. ( 15) and ( 16) means that according to Eq. ( 14), we can now write n R and p R in isolated semiconductors as ( )
div.formula: n p total 0(is) 0(is) R R k np n p = = - ,(17)
div.p: where ( )
div.formula: total bb SRH A k k k k n p = + + + .
div.p: If we can determine 0(is) n and 0(is) p , we can determine 0(bb) G , 0(SRH) G , and 0(A) G . When np 0 RR == , the carrier continuity equations at steady state become

Since there is no current flow (i.e., n J and p J must be zero everywhere inside an isolated semiconductor), n Jx  and p Jx  must also be zero, which means Eqs. ( 18) and ( 19) can be satisfied only if n G and p G are zero. This means that the situation np 0 RR == can only happen in an isolated semiconductor if there is no external photon absorption, which is well known.
To find out 0(is)

n and 0(is) p , we simply need to solve n 0 J = and p 0 J = . An internal electric field can exist inside an isolated semiconductor, for example if the isolated semiconductor is attached to another isolated semiconductor with a different Fermi level, or if the isolated semiconductor is non-uniformly doped. Inside an isolated semiconductor, the electric field F is determined by the gradient of the conduction band minimum c E , given by c F dE qdx = , or by the gradient of the valence band maximum v E , given by v
div.formula: F dE qdx = [2, p. 46].
div.p: When F is not zero, the conditions n 0 J = [refer Eq. ( 6)] and p 0 J = [refer Eq. ( 7)] lead to
div.formula: 0(is) 0(is) c B dn n dE dx k T dx =- , (20
) 0(is) 0(is) v B dp p dE dx k T dx = . (21
div.p: When 0 F = (hence the gradients of c E and v E are zero), the conditions n 0 J = and p 0
div.formula: J = lead to 0(is) 0 dn dx = , (22
div.p: Equations ( 20) and ( 21) are for isolated semiconductors (intrinsic and extrinsic) when there is an electric field inside the semiconductors, whereas Eqs. ( 22) and ( 23) are also for isolated semiconductors (intrinsic and extrinsic) but when there is no electric field inside the semiconductors.

First, we will try to determine 0(is) n and 0(is) p for the intrinsic semiconductor case. We know that 0(is) n and 0(is) p are the electron and the hole concentrations, respectively, when the net carrier recombination rate and the external photon absorption are zero. For an isolated semiconductor in general (i.e., intrinsic and extrinsic), no net carrier recombination and no external photon absorption mean the semiconductor is in thermodynamic equilibrium [1,2].

For an isolated intrinsic semiconductor in thermodynamic equilibrium, the electron concentration and the hole concentration are called the intrinsic electron concentration int n and the intrinsic hole concentration int p , respectively, which are given by [2, p. 20] ( ) Therefore, for both cases (when an electric field does and does not exist), it is clear that the parameters 0(is) n and 0(is) p for an isolated intrinsic semiconductor is given by int n and int p , respectively.
div.formula: c Fi int c B exp EE nN kT --  =   , (24
) ( ) Fi v int v B exp EE pN kT --  =   , (25
div.p: Next, to determine 0(is) n and 0(is) p for the extrinsic semiconductor case, we follow a similar procedure as for the intrinsic case above. First, we need to know the electron and the hole concentrations in isolated extrinsic semiconductors when there are no net carrier recombination and no external photon absorption (i.e., in thermodynamic equilibrium). When an isolated extrinsic semiconductor is in thermodynamic equilibrium, we know the product of the electron and the hole concentrations is still equal to the product of the electron and the hole concentrations before the semiconductor is doped (i.e., int int np) [2, pp. 20-22]. From above, we know that int n and int p can satisfy the conditions given by Eqs. (20), (21), (22), and (23).

Therefore, for an isolated extrinsic semiconductor, 0(is) 0(is) np for both cases (when an electric field does and does not exist) is still given by int int np, where int n and int p are the intrinsic electron and the intrinsic hole concentrations, respectively, inside the extrinsic semiconductor before it is doped.
div.formula: Since int int np = [2, p. 22], this means 2 0(is) 0(is) int n p n =
div.p: . Applying the obtained result to Eqs. ( 9), (11), and (13), the net band-to-band, the net SRH, and the net Auger recombination rates per unit volume in isolated semiconductors (intrinsic and extrinsic) are therefore given by ( )
div.formula: 2 bb bb int R k np n =- ,(26)
2 SRH SRH int R k np n =- ,(27) ( )(
2 A A A int R k n k p np n = + - . (28
div.p: The results given by Eqs. ( 26
div.formula: 2 net r int R k np n =- ,(29)
div.p: where r k is the general coefficient of the considered recombination process. The recombination model given by Eq. ( 29) is the conventional model that is derived (and hence valid) for modeling net R in isolated semiconductors, but the model is also adopted for modeling net R in semiconductor devices. In the next subsection, we will derive the model to describe net R in semiconductor devices and show that the model is not as described by Eq. (29).

div.head: Model for quantifying the net carrier recombination rate in semiconductor devices
div.p: To derive the parameters 0(bb)
When n R and p R are zero, the continuity equations for the carriers inside the considered semiconducting material become

An external voltage bias can be applied to a semiconductor device which can result in non-zero n J and p J . External photon absorption contributes to the excess free carriers. At any given bias condition, n R and p R are expected to reach their lowest value (i.e. zero) when the excess free carriers are at the lowest (i.e. when there is no external photon absorption or np 0 GG ==
). Therefore, at any given bias condition, np 0 RR == should happen when np 0 GG == .
Considering the case when n G and p G are zero, Eqs. (32) and (33) become
Here we show that both 0(is) n (for isolated semiconductors) and that appears in the expressions for n J and p J ) and the relevant boundary conditions at the edges of the considered material.

To obtain the analytical expressions for 0(d) n and 0(d) p , we need to make a simplification by assuming F to be uniform (i.e. independent of x ). Applying the assumption and substituting Eqs. ( 6) and (7) It is worth noting that for the special case when 0 F = , the first terms on the left-hand side of Eqs. ( 36) and (37) disappear, and the general solutions for this special case are
div.formula: 0(d) n n n A x B =+ , (40
) 0(d) p p p A x B =+ . (41
div.p: Applying the obtained result to Eqs. ( 9), (11), and (13), the net band-to-band, the net SRH, and the net Auger recombination rates per unit volume in a semiconducting material of a semiconductor device are therefore given by ( )
div.formula: bb bb 0(d) 0(d) R k np n p =- ,(42)
SRH SRH 0(d) 0(d) R k np n p =- ,(43) ( )(
A A A 0(d) 0(d) R k n k p np n p = + - .(44)
div.p: Based on our result here, we can propose that the net carrier recombination rate per unit volume net R in a semiconductor device can be modeled as
div.formula: ( net r 0(d) 0(d) R k np n p =- ,(45)

div.head: Method
div.p: To support the newly proposed model for describing net R in semiconductor devices (as derived in Sec. 3.2) and discuss its significance, we will compare between the use of the proposed model and the use of the conventional net R model (as derived in Sec. 3.1) in obtaining the current-voltage (J-V) characteristic of a specific semiconductor device, namely organic solar cell (OSC). The reason why OSC is used is because the source code for calculating the J-V characteristic of OSCs is available to us, and hence we can modify the recombination model in the code whether to follow the conventional model or the newly proposed model. The OSC considered here consists of an active layer (a blend of an electron accepting material and an electron donating material) sandwiched between two flat electrodes as described in Ref. [3] and as shown in Fig. 1a. The analytical model developed in Ref. [3] is used here to calculate the J-V characteristics, where the electric field is taken to be uniform and given by a bi
div.formula: VV F L - = , (46
div.p: where a V is the applied voltage bias, and
div.formula: ( ) bi Fc Fa V E E q =-
div.p: is the built-in voltage. The boundary conditions for the electron concentration n and the hole concentration p at the edges of the active layer (at 0 x = and xL = ) in Ref. [3] are given by ( )
div.formula: g pa c 0 B exp x E nN kT  =  -- =    , (47
) nc c B exp xL nN kT  =  - =   , (48
g nc v B exp xL E pN kT  =  -- =    , (50
div.p: where pa  is the hole injection barrier at anode given by the difference between HOMOd and Fa E (refer Fig. 1a), and nc  is the electron injection barrier at cathode given by the difference between LUMOa and Fc E (refer Fig. 1a).

The model in Ref. [3] considers the bimolecular recombination and the monomolecular recombination. The monomolecular recombination is basically a simplified (approximated) version of the SRH recombination and is neglected in our calculations here. The bimolecular recombination (which is basically the band-to-band recombination) is considered in our calculations here. The net bimolecular recombination rate per unit volume in the active layer
div.formula: according to the conventional net R model (see Sec. 3.1) is ( ) 2 b(conv) L int R k np n  =- , (51
div.p: where  is the bimolecular recombination reduction coefficient, ( )
div.formula: L n p kq    =+ is the
div.p: Langevin recombination coefficient with  being the effective permittivity of the active layer, and int n is the intrinsic electron concentration given by Eq. ( 24). The relationship
div.formula: ( ) b(new) L 0(d) 0(d) R k np n p  =- ,(52)
div.p: where 0(d) n is given by Eq. (38) and 0(d) p is given by Eq. (39). For brevity, no calculations at exactly 0 F = (i.e., at a bi VV = ) will be made in this paper, and hence Eqs.
div.formula: c nc B c g pa B n B exp exp exp 1 N k T N E k T A qFL k T   - - - -    = --   ,( ) ( ) ( ) ( )
n c g pa B n exp B N E k T A   = - - -  ,(53) ( ) ( )
v g nc B v pa B p B exp exp exp 1 N E k T N k T A qFL k T    - - - -   = -   ,(54) ( ) ( ) ( ) ( )
p v pa B p exp B N k T A   = - -  . ((55) ( )
div.p: The parameter values used in our calculations here are the same as used in Ref. [3] under the 1 sun condition (which are based on the P3HT:PCBM solar cell in Ref. [4]), except for the value of  [refer Eqs. (51) and ( 52)]. Here, 0.01
div.formula:  = is used compared with 0.001  =
div.p: used in Ref. [3] and Ref. [4]. A reduction in the bimolecular recombination coefficient in comparison with the Langevin recombination coefficient was observed experimentally [5,6].

The reduction is partly because the free electrons and the free holes are located in different materials of the active layer (which consists of donor and acceptor networks), and this reduces the probability for them to meet and recombine [7].  can be affected by the fineness of the donor-acceptor morphology of the interpenetrating networks [8], and can have a value range of 01   . A higher  is used here compared with the value in Ref. [3] and Ref. [4] because we want to obtain results that can adequately distinguish between the use of the conventional model and the new model. shown in Fig. 3), we show that the use of the proposed model is important in order to accurately evaluate the performance of OSCs, and we expect that the use of the proposed model is also important to accurately evaluate the performance of other optoelectronic devices whose performance is influenced by carrier recombination such as hybrid perovskite solar cells and LEDs.
Fig. 4 The same plot as Fig. 3 but focusing on the J-V characteristics from slightly below the short-circuit to slightly above the open-circuit.

Finally, we will attempt to shed some light on the physical process of 0 G [refer Eq. ( 1)] in the semiconducting material of a semiconductor device. As mentioned earlier in Sec. p . When a semiconductor device is under a voltage bias, free carriers from outside the semiconducting material of the device can be forced into the semiconductor. Therefore, in the semiconducting material a semiconductor device, electrons in the conduction band and holes in the valence band can also be created through carrier injection outside the semiconductor, besides through carrier thermal excitation within the semiconductor (electrons jumping from the valence band to the conduction band in the semiconductor). A semiconductor that is part of a semiconductor device must be connected to an external circuit and its components (otherwise the semiconductor is an isolated semiconductor where no electric current can flow), and therefore, carrier generation due to carrier injection should be considered as part of the carrier generation that inherently occurs in a semiconductor that is part of a device. This means that when a device is under a voltage bias, additional free carriers are injected into the semiconducting material of the device, and therefore, 0(d) 0(d) np must be larger than int int np, and this explains the results shown in Fig. 2. Furthermore, when a device is not under an applied bias ( a 0 V = ), no carriers are injected into the semiconducting material of the device, and therefore, 0(d) 0(d) np must be the same as int int np, and this is shown in Fig. 2. Since 0(d) n and 0(d) p exist due to carrier thermal excitation and carrier injection, 0 G in the semiconducting material of a semiconductor device also exist due to carrier thermal excitation and carrier injection (e.g., when a 0 V = and 0 T → , 0 G in a semiconductor device is basically zero even if there is photon absorption).

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: We have investigated the modeling for the net carrier recombination rate in semiconductors.
The net carrier recombination rate per unit volume net R in a semiconductor is conventionally modeled as ( )
div.formula: 2 net r int R k np n =-
div.p: where r k is the general coefficient of the considered recombination process, np is the product of the electron and the hole concentrations in the semiconductor, and int n is the intrinsic electron concentration in the semiconductor. The , respectively, where n J and p J are the electron and the hole current densities in the semiconductor, respectively. By using organic solar cells (OSCs)

as a case study, we analyzed the calculated J-V characteristics and showed that the newly proposed recombination model is the better model for describing net R in OSCs, and this is expected to be the case for other semiconductor devices too. We showed that the use of the proposed model is important in order to accurately evaluate the performance of OSCs, and therefore we expect that the use of the proposed model is also important to better evaluate the performance of other optoelectronic devices whose performance is influenced by carrier recombination such as LEDs. Finally, we have also provided some physical insights on 0(d) n and 0(d) p . We have explained that 0(d) n and 0(d) p are the electron concentration in the conduction band and the hole concentration in the valence band, respectively, that inherently exist in the semiconducting material of a semiconductor device at a given voltage bias, where 0(d) n and 0(d) p can be contributed by the carrier thermal generation from inside the semiconductor as well as by the carrier injection from outside the semiconductor.

figure.figDesc: where r k is the general coefficient of the considered recombination process, 0(d) semiconducting material of the device. Concerning the physical meaning of 0(d) n and 0(d) p , they are simply the electron and the hole concentrations, respectively, that inherently exist in the considered semiconducting material at any given voltage bias when np 0 GG == and np 0 RR == . Further explanation regarding 0(d)

figure.head: Fig. 1 a
figure.figDesc: Fig. 1 a A schematic of the considered OSC showing the positions and the energy levels of the
(40) and (41) will not be used here [i.e., only Eqs. (38) and (39) will be used to describe 0(d)

figure.head: Figure 4
figure.figDesc: Figure4shows that the J-V characteristic obtained using b(new) R is noticeably different

conventional netR model is derived based on a condition that the electric current in the considered semiconductor is always zero, which is valid only if the semiconductor is not part of a semiconductor device. To produce a better and more realistic model for describing net R in semiconductor devices, we have derived and proposed a new model, where the model was derived by considering the fact that electric current can exist in the semiconducting materials of semiconductor devices. We found that the net carrier recombination rate per volume net R in a semiconductor that is part of a semiconductor device can be modeled as is the general coefficient of the considered recombination process, and 0(d) n and 0(d) p are carrier concentrations in the semiconductor that result from the

figure.head: 3 Models for quantifying the net carrier recombination rate

row.cell: In Sec. 3.1, we will rederive the conventional model for quantifying net R . The conventional
model is derived based on semiconductors that are not part of semiconductor devices (called
isolated semiconductors here). In Sec. 3.2, we will derive a new model for quantifying net R
based on semiconductors that are part of semiconductor devices. The purpose we rederive the
conventional net R model is to assist us in deriving and understanding the newly proposed net R
model.
the total band-to-band recombination rate), SRH k np (the total SRH recombination rate), and
( k n p p n 22 A +
(the total Auger recombination rate). Furthermore, the total 0 G is the sum of
0(bb) G
, 0(SRH) G
, and 0(A) G . Since the total rate of a given recombination process cannot be less
than the inherent carrier generation rate (or 0 G ) for that recombination process, this means
bb R  , SRH 0 0 R  and A 0 R  . Therefore, when np 0 RR == , this means bb R , SRH R , and A R
must all be zero since bb R , SRH R , or A R cannot be negative.

figure.figDesc: 1, 0 Gis the generation rate of free electrons (electrons in the conduction band) and free holes (holes in the valence band) per unit volume that inherently or permanently occurs in a given semiconductor. In isolated semiconductors, it is well known that 0 G exists due to thermal agitation[1, pp. 313-324], which can be explained as follows. The product of the free electron and the free hole concentrations that inherently exist in an isolated semiconductor is int int np.

row.cell: As shown in this paper, the significance of 0(d) 0(d) np
for semiconductor devices is
equivalent to the significance of int int np (or
2 n ) for isolated semiconductors. This means int
0(d) 0(d) np
is the product of the free electron and the free hole concentrations that inherently
exist in a semiconductor that is part of a semiconductor device at a given voltage bias, where
the detail can be explained as follows. As can be seen in Eqs. (38), (39), (53), (54), (55), and
(56), carrier injection (represented by the injection barriers and the electric field
F that appear
in the mentioned equations, where
F depends on a V ) contributes to 0(d) n
and 0(d)
This means the total rate for any given recombination process that inherently occurs in an
isolated semiconductor is r int int k n p where r k is the general coefficient of the considered
recombination process [see Eq. (29)]. Since the free electron and the free hole concentrations
must be maintained (i.e., the carrier concentrations must not keep increasing or reducing), this
means that 0 G in an isolated semiconductor must also be the same as the inherent carrier loss
rate, which is r int int k n p , and this explains why we obtain 0(is) 0(is) n p
int int n p
. It is worth noting
that 0 G depends on r k [which may depend on external factors such as photon absorption as
discussed in Eq. (11)] since the rate of inherent carrier generation must match the rate of
inherent carrier recombination, which may depend on external factors, in order to maintain int n
and int p . Since int n and int p exist due to thermal excitation [refer Eq. (24) and (25)], then 0 G
in an isolated semiconductor also simply exist due to carrier thermal excitation (e.g. if
T →
, then int n and int p are basically zero, and this makes 0 G in an isolated semiconductor to be
zero even if there is photon absorption).

div.head: Acknowledgments
div.p: MLII acknowledges the support from the Ministry of Higher Education of Malaysia for the Fundamental Research Grant Scheme (FRGS/1/2017/STG02/UIAM/03/2). Support for AZ work was provided from the Ministry of Education and Science of the Russian Federation (Project 14.Y26.31.0010) and the Russian Science Foundation (№ 19-73-30023), and partial financial support by Welch Foundation grant AT 1617 is also highly appreciated.

div.head: Results and discussion
div.p: n at short-circuit point (at a 0 V = V as shown in Fig. 2b) but not at a non-zero bias (e.g. at a 0.5 V = V as also shown in Fig. 2b), which will be explained at the end of this section. As mentioned in Sec. 3.2, unlike 0(d) n and 0(d) p , int n has no dependency on F (and hence on a V ), and therefore is invariant when a V is varied. Figure 3 compares the J-V characteristic obtained using the proposed recombination model [using b(new) R given by Eq. ( 52)] with the J-V characteristic obtained using the conventional recombination model [using b(conv) R given by Eq. ( 51)]. The J-V characteristic obtained b(new) R gives the expected J-V trend as a V is increased, but the J-V characteristic obtained using b(conv) R gives the "wrong" J-V trend as a V is increased starting from a V just below bi V . The result in Fig. 3 suggests that the newly proposed recombination model is the better model for quantifying the net carrier recombination rate in the semiconducting material of a semiconductor device.

Fig. 3 The J-V characteristics of the OSC from slightly below the short-circuit to slightly above the built-in voltage bi V ( bi V here is 0.8 V, the same as in Ref. [3]). The triangles denote the J- V characteristic obtained using b(conv) R [Eq. ( 51)] and the circles denote the J-V characteristic obtained using b(new) R [Eq. ( 52)].
